dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, you are dispatched to a sick person at [CITY] Apt # E.
Ma'am, I'm Alex and this is my partner, Max. What made you call 911 today?
She doesn't talk right, threw up, and can't use her left arm or side of face.
Ma'am, can you open your eyes for me?
How many fingers am I holding up? Okay, good.
What is your name?
She's Joyce. Usually a very smart lady... well, beyond her dementia.
Now, ma'am, squeeze my finger with your right hand. Strong; now try your left. Her right works fine, but the left is weak.
Airway is clear, no blockages or difficulty. Breathing appears normal.
Ma'am, we're applying an oxygen mask now - it's for safety. Respirations are normal depth and evenly distributed.
Her breathing is at 12 per minute, regular pattern observed.
Her SpO₂ is 92. It's slightly low, but stable for now.
Pulse present, strong and regular in the right arm; left arm weak and skin is diaphoretic.
Mr. Joyce, when did you first notice the changes today?
She started vomiting around 2:30 and since then she just... collapsed in the bathroom.
Ma'am, can you point if it hurts anywhere?
She's not talking clearly and lost the left side of her body again. She just sat like that.
Ma'am, can you or your family tell me your medical history?
She has high blood pressure, hyperlipidemia, cardiac pacemaker, and dementia.
Ma'am, how much pain would you say you are in right now, on a scale of 1 to 10?
No pain. She doesn't seem to be in physical pain at all.
Joyce, what county are we in?
She can't speak properly and doesn't seem to know we're in [CITY] County.
Skin is pale, warm, and diaphoretic overall. Left side appears more so.
Her left arm is floppy, can't hold or lift anything - left-sided weakness noted.
She is incontinent and covered in vomit. We will address hygiene once transport is secured.
Left-side facial droop is noticeable when I compare both sides - indicative of stroke.
Pulse oximeter shows 92.
Her pulse is 75 - it's strong and regular.
Respirations are at 12, regular and even - no signs of distress.
Blood pressure - 133 over 98 - taken on the right arm.
Showing sinus rhythm per our initial EKG with no ST changes.
Blood sugar - reading 172 right now.
Reading capnography now - quick check for airway confirmation.
Capnography at 45 now, expected and remains normal.
Starting 4-lead EKG monitoring as part of stroke protocol.
Applying 3 LPM oxygen via nasal cannula - should help with SpO2.
Partner, protocol implementation for potential stroke - call [CITY] with emergency alert.
Pulse now is 88. Increased slightly, but still regular.
Respiratory rate is 19 per minute, still within normal limits.
Blood pressure is 193 over 92 - significant increase from baseline.
SpO2 is at 93 - a slight improvement from initial reading.
Reran 4-lead EKG - still sinus rhythm with no changes noted.
Blood glucose appears undetectable, possibly due to vomiting and decreased oral intake.
Reran ECG - still stable sinus rhythm, repeat capnography 45 again - no changes.
Mr. Joyce, has her pain level changed from our initial check?
No, same. Still no verbal response, no indication of pain.
Any new medications given around the time of worsening?
No. Her usual is Celexa, donepezil, Lisinopril, and Ativan.
Joyce, we need your consent to transport you to [COMPANY_NAME]. You mind?
We'll go to [CITY].
Joyce is en route to [CITY] for stroke alert. I just re-reviewed vitals and she's still meeting criteria.
Help here, we're transferring out. Assistance needed due to left-sided weakness.
We're positioning the scoop stretcher at the angle for safe lift.
Left arm is secured and restraints are being connected to prevent falls.
She is now safe on the stretcher. En route, we'll avoid sharp turns for stability.
We're pre-alerting the hospital for stroke protocol.

ED, we transferred Joyce from oro SCO 3 to [CITY] for stroke alert. She has aphasia, left-sided weakness (hemiplegia), facial droop, GCS 11. She met criteria for a code stroke hospital. Capnography at 45, 4-lead EKG showed normal rhythm, SpO2 improved with O2. Field impression is consistent.
Hospital was alerted. She is stable for transport.
Joyce is now being taken to the hospital CT room for evaluation.
All field procedures and records have been turned over; care is complete.
Thank you so very much for what you did.
No problem - please check back with hospital for updates.
Due to aphasia, left-sided hemiplegia, and GCS 11, field impression is stroke (CVA).
Joyce was safely placed onto hospital transfer cot by scoop stretcher.
She's been transferred to hospital bed with radio alert. Hospital staff are alert for CT.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the appropriate site, given their capacity for stroke care and CTA.
Understood, thanks. We'll see you there.
